entries.problem: A feature branch deployment was failing with HTTP 500 errors caused by Redis connection timeouts (`django_redis.exceptions.ConnectionInterrupted: Redis TimeoutError: Timeout writing to socket`). The smoke tests were hitting the previous release of the feature branch still running in the namespace, which was sending that old instance into a frenzy of Redis disconnect errors during the rolling deploy.
entries.solution: The CloudOps engineer nuked the feature Kubernetes namespace entirely and redeployed from scratch. This cleared the stale previous-release containers that were causing the Redis timeout cascade during the smoke test phase of the Jenkins deploy pipeline.

entries.problem: A user needed to set up message archiving for AU Braze, which required an IAM user with specific S3 permissions for a newly created S3 bucket, but lacked permissions to create IAM users themselves.
entries.solution: A CloudOps engineer noted that IAM roles (not IAM users) should be used instead, following the same approach used for a similar US Braze setup in 2022. The engineer also flagged that the S3 bucket was created in the wrong AWS account (Legacy Production, which is being deprecated) and recommended moving it to a Global-Production account. The user was directed to submit a proper ticket on the CloudOps Requests board for the work to be completed.

rawMessages.text:

I'm trying to set up message archiving for AU Braze, which requires an IAM user with specific S3 permissions. I did create an S3 bucket (`au-braze-message-archive`) but don't seem to have permissions to create IAM users myself.

Could someone help create an IAM user and attach the following (in thread) policy to it?

Hi Aaron, not in front of my laptop yet, but if I remember correctly from having supported the US side with a similar setup a couple years ago, it should support the use of IAM roles instead. We try at all costs to avoid using and maintaining IAM users. Is there a specific set of instructions you were provided? I can take a closer look when I sit down in a while.

Hi again Aaron, confirming I did in-fact perform a similar setup for the US side back in 2022, but with an IAM role (that has the exact same policy you provided). So I know it should be possible.

I see you created that new bucket `tmf-au-braze-message-archive` in the Legacy Production account. Ideally we should refrain from creating any new resources in that account as we still have aspirations to close it at some point. I'm guessing you did it there because it is the only account you have access to. To avoid having to migrate it later, I think a better spot for this would be one of the 3 Global-dedicated accounts, probably Global-Production.

Happy to help you further with this if you want to pop a card on our CloudOps Requests board.
Hi Tom, Yes, sorry, that was the only account that showed up for me. I've not really done anything with my Fool AWS account before.  I'll add a proper ticket  in the board.
No worries!

entries.problem: A user gets a '413 Request Entity Too Large' error when trying to access Rancher via Okta SSO tile, preventing them from accessing staging pods.
entries.solution: Using incognito mode resolved the issue. The root cause was likely bloated cookies from the browser. Additional suggestions included clearing site cookies and checking for competing VPNs or tunnels (e.g., Tailscale) that can inflate request size and conflict with ZScaler.

I've tried in 2 different browsers (Brave & Safari), but have not tried incognito mode yet; I will now though :slightly_smiling_face:
Yikes! ok, let me see what happens if I go to rancher
I was able to log in -- you might also try clearing all <http://fool.com|fool.com> cookies?
Or DM me if I can run something for ya
incognito worked :shrug:
pesky cookies :cookie-monster:

Also any other competing VPNs or tunnels (either on your network or on the computer) can cause this. 
Tailscale has bloated my requests before (I know you are a fellow home labber so just be aware that ZScaler no likey) :+1:

entries.problem: A user needed an API key for incident.io to enable on-call rotation automation in Lumos (for Slack channels and escalated access management), and was unsure whether to request it via a cloudops ticket or sysadmin.
entries.solution: Both cloudops and sysadmin have owner access to incident.io, so either team can fulfill the request. The required scopes were identified as: view public incidents and organization settings, read schedules, and view catalog types and entries. The API key was created with the description 'Lumos integration - On call access' and credentials were shared via DM.

entries.problem: The admin-ui service is experiencing intermittent ReadTimeout errors (5s timeout) when calling the Auth0 custom domain endpoint (auth.fool.com) in production. The issue occurs inconsistently from the same pod, while local calls succeed, and Auth0's status page eventually showed green. The timeouts occur when calls take just over 5 seconds, while successful calls complete in 200-600ms.
entries.solution: Investigation is still ongoing; Auth0 support is reviewing timestamps. A proposed mitigation is to add retry logic with exponential backoff to the admin-ui Auth0 client for transient read failures, using a Retry configuration such as: Retry(total=3, connect=3, read=3, status=3, backoff_factor=0.5, status_forcelist=[500, 502, 503, 504]). DNS resolution of the custom domain was also verified as correct.

rawMessages.text:
admin-ui is getting intermittent timeouts calling <http://auth.fool.com|auth.fool.com> (auth0 alias). Auth0 status is green, and I am able to make the same call from the same place (rancher in the admin-ui web pod) sometimes but not consistently. I can make the same call locally fine. is there any chance there is something up with connections on prod?
```    raise ReadTimeout(e, request=request)
requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='<http://auth.fool.com|auth.fool.com>', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=5) ```
As you noted in the <#C018YDP0L6A> thread, it appears Auth0 finally updated their <https://status.auth0.com/|status page> to indicate there are reported issues
Hey Donald, I see they updated the status to indicate the issues have been resolved. Let us know if you continue to see timeouts.

We are still seeing a lot of timeouts actually. I have a support ticket with Auth0; they said their incident was fully resolved so this is not related to that incident, but they are going to look at the timestamps of the timeouts and see if they can find anything on their end. In the meantime, is it worth having a look on our side to see if it could be some connection issue with our infra?

here are logs of the incidents for the past 7 days, more than I had thought originally <https://app.datadoghq.com/logs?query=service%3Aadmin-ui%20status%3Aerror%20%22Error%20creating%20Auth0%20password%20reset%20link%20for%22&agg_m=count&agg_m_source=base&agg_t=count&cols=host%2Cservice&flat_group_bys=true&messageDisplay=inline&refresh_mode=sliding&sort_m=count&sort_t=count&storage=hot&stream_sort=time%2Cdesc&top_n=10&top_o=top&viz=stream&x_missing=true&from_ts=1779891538473&to_ts=1780496338473&live=true|https://app.datadoghq.com/logs?query=service%3Aadmin-ui%20status%3Aerror%20%22Error%2[…]ing=true&from_ts=1779891538473&to_ts=1780496338473&live=true>
Thanks for the update Donald. Tagging in <@U07DF9NFCD7> and <@U07LF0S5CR4> for some extra eyes on this.

Public and private dns both have <http://auth.fool.com|auth.fool.com> pointing to <http://fool-cd-duepoq1bo4cqhjpd.edge.tenants.auth0.com|fool-cd-duepoq1bo4cqhjpd.edge.tenants.auth0.com>, I'm assuming that is still correct?

:thinking_face: I thought it just mapped to `<http://fool.auth0.com|fool.auth0.com>`  but beyond that I don't know. I just read through their documentation <https://auth0.com/docs/customize/custom-domains?tenant=fool%40prod-us-1&locale=en-us|here> on custom domains and I can't find anything that seems relevant about the actual final hostname resolution...

I've been having a long conversation with ChatGPT about this, and in the course of doing that and looking at data points in DD (particularly <https://app.datadoghq.com/apm/traces?agg_m_a=count&agg_m_source_a=base&agg_t_a=count&cols=core_service%2Ccore_resource_name%2Clog_duration%2Clog_http.method%2Clog_http.status_code&colsTraces=service%2C%40duration%2C%40_span.count%2Cresource_name%2C%40_duration.by_service&fromUser=false&graphType=service_map&historicalData=true&messageDisplay=inline&query_a=service%3Aadmin-ui%20env%3Aproduction%20%40http.method%3APOST%20%40http.url%3A%22https%3A%2F%2Fadmin.fool.com%2Fmembers%2Fauth0-admin%22&query_translation_version=v0&shouldShowLegend=true&sort=desc&spanType=all&spanViewType=metadata&storage=hot&target-span=a&trace_group_by_from=root&traceQuery=a&view=traces&viz=stream&start=1780418612269&end=1780505012269&paused=false|traces>), I'm seeing that this read timeout occurs when the call to <http://auth.fool.com|auth.fool.com> takes more than 5 seconds (often just slightly north of that number). In contrast, all successful calls tend to take anywhere from 200-600ms.

The code for admin-ui currently sets the timeout value to 5 seconds.
<https://github.com/themotleyfool/admin-ui/blob/bda452a1e195b843ae67f60cbe70b6529a08f5c4/member_admin/objects/auth0/client.py#L103>

One suggestion, among a few, from ChatGPT, is to add retry support for transient read failures. Something like the following instead of <https://github.com/themotleyfool/admin-ui/blob/bda452a1e195b843ae67f60cbe70b6529a08f5c4/member_admin/objects/auth0/client.py#L93-L99|this>.
```Retry(
    total=3,
    connect=3,
    read=3,
    status=3,
    backoff_factor=0.5,
    status_forcelist=[500, 502, 503, 504],
)```
Here is my ChatGPT <https://chatgpt.com/share/6a20646e-e390-83ea-a088-74809570dfca|transcript> thus far in case it makes any lightbulbs go off with your more in-depth knowledge of the admin-ui app and auth0 env.

entries.problem: A GitHub Actions workflow needs to auto-update package.json version to match a pushed git tag, but the Actions bot cannot push directly to the main branch due to branch protection rules. This causes publish failures when the package.json version is not manually bumped before release.
entries.solution: Two recommended approaches: (1) Use `npm version --no-git-tag-version "$VERSION"` in the workflow to set the version from the tag and build/publish from the working tree without committing. (2) Have the workflow create a branch, update package.json, and open a PR automatically. To handle branch protections requiring approval, use an internal bot user combined with a workflow that auto-approves and merges PRs only when changes are limited to package version updates (e.g., using tj-actions/changed-files to detect package changes).

rawMessages.text: hi cloudops -- procedure/standard practice question on GH Actions -- i am trying to have a step that auto-updates package.json package version metadata to match the gh tag when a tag is pushed, but the gh action bot does not have automatic rights to push back to master when I have branch protections turned on. Is there a standard way we should use around this, or should I just go with whatever I can get to work?
Can you have it put a PR in? Otherwise you need to update the branch protection rules to allow direct pushes to main

Hi Donald, I was about to say what Logan just said, but best practice is usually not to let GH actions bypass branch protections and push directly to master.

An alternative approach is to have automation instead open a PR, such as the following workflow:
1. Tag pushed
2. Action updates package.json
3. Action creates a branch
4. Action opens PR
5. Normal protection/reviews apply
6. Merge PR
If the goal is simply to have package.json match the release tag, a cleaner solution could be something like this:
```- name: Set package version from tag
  run: |
    VERSION=${GITHUB_REF_NAME#v}
    npm version --no-git-tag-version "$VERSION"```
And then build/publish from that working tree without committing.
or something similar

ok, that's kind of what I thought and where I was heading. I might just remove the branch protections for now. The goal was to just let the gh tag be the source of the published version in code artifact automatically without having to remember to manually bump the package.jsonversion and/or do extra merges. (actually sounds like just auto-creating another PR then merging before publish is the best answer)
thanks! :thanks2: :brain:
Out of curiosity, which repo is this for?
fool-auth-js
i almost always publish a new release but forget to bump the package.json version so it fails to publish and I have to go through the whole publish process again

In global we have branch protection turned on including requiring an approval, so to solve a similar issue we have a workflow that automatically approves and merges PRs only when the changes are for package versions and not from a bot.

Something like that combined with the suggestion from Chilly would do it, I think.

entries.problem: A team is adding 3 new tables (`apx_raw_closed`, `apx_closed`, `apx_portfolio_codes`) to a shared PostgreSQL database managed via Django ORM, as part of a modernization effort. CloudOps needs to review the change to ensure it won't break replication or cause performance issues on the shared database cluster.
entries.solution: The changes were reviewed and deemed acceptable: bulk inserts (batch size ~1k) for ~10k rows are expected to complete in seconds and won't stress the database cluster or spike transaction logs. Adding new tables does not break replication in general. Recommendations were made to chunk reads into 1-2k batches as data grows, and a note was added to a ticket about future investigation into using the read-only replica endpoint for batch jobs.

rawMessages.text:
Hi, in the context of APX modernization, we'll create 3 new tables:
• `apx_raw_closed`
• `apx_closed`
• `apx_portfolio_codes`
 They're managed through Django ORM: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/pull/4145>.
We'll use them to run the new process (API) in parallel to the old process (Airflow + stored procedures) and compare the results. Once all is fine for a month, we'll switch over to the new process.
<@U050HGZDZMX> :point_up::skin-tone-3:
For more context, this is in the `fool-iq-insights` `fooliq` postgres database.

Hey, sorry that I wasn't able to get to this until now. It's a huge PR. I probably won't be able to really understand what's going on. Correct me if I'm wrong, you want to have tables in fooliq prod database that will be populated with data from APX API. I'm more concerned about recurring data loads. Here are my questions:

1. When saving data to the database, especially for recurring jobs, is the data saved row by row or in a batch? Can you point me to the code? I'd like writing to the database to be batched, if it's not already.
2. How much data (rows) per day are we looking to write?
3. Once batched, roughly how long does it need to write to the database (excluding the time waiting for API calls to return)?
4. For `compare_apx_closed` and `transform_apx_closed` commands, are the reads from and writes to the database batched? Does the read use an index? If it's just a handful of unoptimized batched queries running daily in a reasonable amount of time, I'm not too concerned.

> you want to have tables in fooliq prod database that will be populated with data from APX API
Correct.

> saved row by row or in a batch?
Bulk: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/pull/4145/changes#diff-a040d0446e0bf5a0f3478501751b5e10fde3c6a053a1d186bc41f17edff0094dR32>

> How much data (rows) per day are we looking to write?
There are currently ~7k closed and ~2k open rows. So, 10k-ish rows total? Current job runs every 15 minutes, I think.

> Once batched, roughly how long does it need to write to the database
Don't know, will need to run on production database to get an accurate metric.

> compare_apx_closed
It just gets all rows (because it has to), but again, not a lot of rows: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/pull/4145/changes#diff-90917938e2db2f87502a52fcaf70b54a74466ae21e28b396e9367cdb2a3b544fR89-R90>. No writes, just reads.

> transform_apx_closed
There is an order by `extracted_at` which has an index on it already: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/pull/4145/changes#diff-ec45fc76f849d58ffd661e22322137595238e0cf1bae19e1dbd8d1be747abee6R252>. Writes are bulk: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/pull/4145/changes#diff-a040d0446e0bf5a0f3478501751b5e10fde3c6a053a1d186bc41f17edff0094dR51-R52>.

Maybe I misunderstood, but I thought the reason to alert #cloud-ops for changes to the `fooliq` Postgres database was to not break replication. Adding new tables to `fooliq` should not break replication, correct?

This is very helpful. Adding new tables won't break replication in general, but if it's a very write-heavy (in the GBs) table, that may spike database cluster transaction logs given the current setup. Once we move off of Striim, that won't be an issue anymore.

Yes, checking if changes break replication is the minimal check, but I'd like to go a little further and check a few obvious performance considerations while I can, as the database cluster is shared with other apps. It's a lot easier to catch and fix any potential performance issues now than later. You probably notice I ask almost the same questions. I'll scale back on those performance checks in the future.

> There are currently ~7k closed and ~2k open rows. So, 10k-ish rows total? Current job runs every 15 minutes,
That should be fine given the batch size is 1k. Just 10 writes total.

If it's 10k rows inserted, then the writes should finish in seconds. So I'm not concerned unless you guys find out it takes long. Please still do test.

> It just gets all rows
We talking about in the Ks, 10Ks or 100Ks? Does that grow over time? Actually, are you even able to hold all that in memory if that number gets too large? Is there any possibility to explicitly point to the read endpoint, if possible? If it's less than 10k and doesn't grow, then probably not worth it.

That's pretty much it for now. Thanks again for checking with me.

> We talking about in the Ks, 10Ks or 100Ks? Does that grow over time?
There will be either ~2k or ~7k rows (depends on the type of recommendation type). So, definitely under 10k, however it will grow slow over time as we add new recommendations to portfolios.

> Is there any possibility to explicitly point to the read endpoint, if possible?
Do you mean the read-only replica? Yeah, that would doable -- I actually have a spike to dig into this at some point (<https://fool.atlassian.net/browse/ITK-455>), however it would require a little bit of work I think. I am a little unsure what the performance specs are for that replica, how much load it can handle, etc.

Introducing the read-only database cluster endpoint for the entire app is a bit tricky. I tried and failed before. I'm thinking of using the read-only db endpoint for batch jobs as a start.

Going to leave this information in that ticket.

I've attempted to use the read-only database cluster endpoint in fool-iq-insights for the app by introducing the database router, but failed.

Here is my attempt.

feat: use db read replica
<https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/pull/3137/changes>

Here is the removal of the database router. Check the history of `db_router.py` file. It's a bit tricky to get right.

fix: remove database router
<https://github.com/themotleyfool/fool-iq-insights/commit/0e893a7232bf5067b7d61aef9f58b38e33345c7b>

> Db router creates complications that may have triggered the auto vacuum
> and table lockups for writes. This could be coming from custom post save
> actions.
I think we also cache the pickled ORM objects in Redis. That's likely a blocker too because the ORM objects maintain which db endpoint it was created from.

> There will be either ~2k or ~7k rows (depends on the type of recommendation type). So, definitely under 10k, however it will grow slow over time as we add new recommendations to portfolios.
I think this is okay for now. I would recommend chunking the reads into 1-2k chunks if that doesn't take much time to do. How slow is slow growth, roughly speaking?

> How slow is slow growth, roughly speaking?
Depends on how many portfolios are currently hot.
I'm eyeballing here. Around 1k a year is the upper bound. If you can chunk the reads, then you won't need to worry about this later.

> Going to leave this information in that ticket.
Thanks! Yeah, this was mostly a shower thought, I didn't do any research into how hard it would be.

entries.problem: A developer was confused about which Dockerfile is used in the new NextJS GitHub Actions based deployment workflow, unsure how the path `./Dockerfile` in the workflow file resolved compared to the legacy `cicd/docker` path.
entries.solution: The workflow uses a `Dockerfile` located at the root of the repository. The path `./Dockerfile` is relative to the repository root, not the `cicd/docker` directory. The `cicd` folder contains legacy files from old Helm charts and the Jenkins pipeline, which will be deleted once apps are fully migrated to the new pipeline.

The Github Action can use a Dockerfile in any directory, just needs the path to it. In this case it's using this one: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/sanity-cms/blob/master/Dockerfile>

The `cicd` folder is full of legacy stuff related to old helm charts and the Jenkins pipeline. We've been making a clean break from it and when apps are fully migrated and running on the new pipeline the idea is to just delete that folder from the repo.

entries.problem: When users add redirects in the Billboard/Cloudflare tool, trailing slashes are auto-removed from URLs before saving. If a user's browser cached the URL with a trailing slash, the redirect rule won't match and the redirect won't fire.
entries.solution: The team agreed to store both forms of each URL (with and without trailing slash) as separate redirect entries in Cloudflare, accepting the doubled list size as the least-resistance short-term fix. A longer-term path involves working with dev teams to remove non-slash to slash 308 redirects in NextJS, which would also resolve the underlying issue.

rawMessages.text:
Hi team - I'm not sure if this is a good solution so wanted your thoughts *re: saving redirects in Cloudflare* (aka Billboard tool):
• When users add new redirects, the *trailing slash gets auto removed* and the source -> target urls are saved to Cloudlfare
• It is possible that any of those urls were already cached by a user's browser and they auto added a trailing slash, so the above rule will not hit and the redirect won't happen
    ◦ we can start saving urls *with and without trailing slashes* so we don't run into the above issue - this will double our list and we might hit limits - any thoughts on a different approach for this?

or can do this:
```Three ways:
1. Flip normalization to add a trailing slash for <http://fool.com|fool.com> source URLs (matches the site's canonical form). Cleanest single fix.
2. Store both forms per redirect (slash + no-slash entries). Most explicit, no edge cases, but doubles list entries.
3. Enable Subpath matching on the entry in Cloudflare (no code change) — but it also catches …/index-fund/anything, so only safe if no child pages exist.```
#2 is the least path of resistance right now. I'll work with the dev teams to see if there's a path forward on removing the non-slash to slash 308 redirects for NextJS which would also fix this

entries.problem: A user was unable to query Snowflake locally while on VPN, being blocked by ZScaler.
entries.solution: A ZScaler profile/policy was missing for the user. An admin identified the block in ZScaler logs, submitted an access request on the user's behalf, and once approved by another admin, the user was able to connect to Snowflake via VPN. Requests are handled on a case-by-case basis.

The request was approved. Give it another try.

entries.problem: After a CI/CD cutover, a CMS application began receiving 403 errors when accessing AWS. The Kubernetes namespace changed from 'sanity-cms-{environment}' to 'sanity-cms', and the service account in the new namespace references an IAM role ('sanity-cms-k8s') that does not exist in AWS. The old deployment used a differently named role created by the previous CI/CD system.
entries.solution: The fix requires creating the missing IAM role ('sanity-cms-k8s') for the new namespace. The role creation logic exists in the helm chart templates and a Terraform EKS service account role module, so a PR was being prepared to provision the missing role. A Jira ticket (CLOPS-3779) was created to track this work. As an interim measure, the old deployment can continue running alongside the new one sharing the same SQS queue until the role is created and validated.

entries.problem: Kubernetes health probes on EKS pods running a Next.js app were hitting a streaming page, causing the connection to be closed mid-stream and generating high volumes of Sentry errors (TypeError: controller[kState].transformAlgorithm is not a function).
entries.solution: A dedicated static health check endpoint at /api/healthcheck was created to return { status: 'ok' } with no streaming. The Helm chart values.yaml in the app repo was updated to point liveness/readiness probes to /api/healthcheck (with trailing slash /api/healthcheck/ to avoid 308 redirects caused by Next.js middleware trailing-slash redirect logic). The change was tested in a feature environment and a PR was submitted before rolling out to production.

rawMessages.text:
Hey team, we're seeing a high volume of errors in Sentry (TypeError: controller[kState].transformAlgorithm is not a function) coming from our foolcom pods on EKS. After investigating, we believe the root cause is that k8s health probes are hitting a streaming Next.js page, closing the connection mid-stream, and that's what's causing the error.

To fix it, we've added a dedicated static health check endpoint at /api/healthcheck that returns { status: "ok" } with no streaming involved.

Two questions:
  1. What are you currently looking at for health checks on the foolcom pods?
  2. Can you point them to /api/healthcheck instead?

yes, the healthcheck endpoints can be updated in your helm <https://github.com/themotleyfool/helm-charts/blob/56bfeaed2c1494676de092901cbb67108477a6dd/charts/react-app/values-all-enabled.yaml#L65|values.yaml>

These are all the available configs: <https://github.com/themotleyfool/helm-charts/blob/56bfeaed2c1494676de092901cbb67108477a6dd/charts/react-app/values-all-enabled.yaml#L65>

and

<https://github.com/themotleyfool/helm-charts/blob/56bfeaed2c1494676de092901cbb67108477a6dd/charts/react-app/values-all-enabled.yaml#L74>
They should have the same paths
Thank you

<https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/trailingSlash>

entries.problem: User was unsure whether feature environment deployments still require a branch name starting with 'feature' prefix when using the new GitHub Actions based workflows.
entries.solution: With GitHub Actions, any branch name can be built and deployed to the feature environment manually — the 'feature' prefix is no longer required.

rawMessages.text: With the new GitHub actions based workflows do feature environments still require that a branch start with `feature` ? I know we don't auto deploy them anymore but I wasn't sure if we still need the prefix to trigger a build.
Any branch name can be built and deployed to `feature` via GH actions manually

entries.problem: The staging registration API could not connect to the Party database, failing with 'Login failed for user PartyUser'. The issue began on a Sunday and appeared related to a database backup/restore job, where the restore reported success but the user databases were not actually present on the staging and dev instances.
entries.solution: A DBA investigated and found that despite the restore jobs reporting success, the user databases were missing from the staging and dev SQL instances. A completely new backup set was created and the restore jobs were re-run manually. Once the restore completed successfully, the database credentials worked again and the staging API resumed normal operation. A rewrite of the brittle backup/restore process was planned, including better error/retry logic and Slack notifications for success and failure.

rawMessages.text: hi cloudops! _*staging*_ reg api is not able to connect to party DB, possibly partyUser credentials are not working, probably since Sunday -- was there a recent change or possibly sync job error?
<@U05TF4TPR6Y>  :point_up::skin-tone-3:
no changes

(FYI some <https://app.datadoghq.com/logs?query=service%3Aregistration-api-core%20env%3Astaging%20status%3Aerror&agg_m=count&agg_m_source=base&agg_t=count&cols=host%2Cservice&messageDisplay=inline&refresh_mode=sliding&storage=hot&stream_sort=desc&viz=stream&from_ts=1779546232628&to_ts=1779805432628&live=true|DD logs>
```---> Microsoft.Data.SqlClient.SqlException (0x80131904): Cannot open database "Party" requested by the login. The login failed. Login failed for user 'PartyUser'.```
we haven't had any re-deploys for a few weeks so the credentials in the app should be the same
it looks like an auth error
<@U050HGZDZMX> any ideas?
<@U055Z0SEYMN> could this be related to the db restore job?
I saw the staging internal endpoint failure yesterday.

it looks like these were successful but definitely that date corresponds with the connection error starting -- can we just re-run the restore job and see, or does this require some manual intervention? <https://github.com/themotleyfool/tmf-sql-workflows/actions/runs/26356751784>
(kind of curious that no other DBs are having same issue though...)

we have been having trouble with the MS SQL native backups running to completion on prod, which is done Sunday mornings, but a failed backup will block a restore and the previous dev/staging MS SQL data  is not touched
I added logging to the process and will go take a look to see if it failed
just confirmed both restore jobs (05/24) were successful, which means the backup job was also successful

> restore jobs (05/24) were successful
does this mean that the credentials issue is not related? how do we get our staging credentials working so that reg api can talk to party DB?
(or is there potentially a different connection issue that would cause `Login failure for user 'PartyUser'`?)
Backup / Restore process only touches user databases, creds are in the system database, so I wouldn't think it would be related, where are your creds stored, I can check them on the instance
*`apps/registration-api-core`* has been untouched ; only the `Encrypt=False;` worries me
but it’s also false on prod
this used to happen all the time on restores and Manaen would have to go in and do something to re-enable the DB user or something... the creds have not chnaged
i cannot connect with creds that used to work last week. the errors started on sunday, seems to me likely related
yeah, makes sense, I bet the map between the user and the tables has been broken
give me a few to login and check it out

well, interesting enough the restores says they succeeded, but upon logging into both staging and dev instances, the user databases are not there, the backups did succeed, so I am re-running the two restore jobs now - staging first
looks like that completed but my staging credentials still are not working on manually connecting and reg api still throwing 500's with same errors....
the new restore just started, I had to do a completely new backup set
you can track progress of this job - <https://github.com/themotleyfool/tmf-sql-workflows/actions/runs/26466469843>
ok that completed successfully, can you check and let me know - then I will fire up dev restore
looks good! :+1:  i can call users endpoints now and staging credentials work for me
cool - updating dev next - I am going to keep my eyes on this process - it is scheduled for a rewrite - it is somewhat brittle and needs better error/retry logic

Sorry to jump in randomly, but curious if there's a way to prevent this from happening in the future, or some kind of way to document and/or create an automation to resolve this if it happens again? I'm not understanding the problem as well as I could, so maybe that's the issue, too lol

the backup and restore processes are not robust, so the solution is to rewrite them - that will start next week - I will document the process in the repo that will run the scripts and there will be slack notification for both success and failure
Amazing, thanks for the info!
CloudOps staging endpoints and playwright tests are passing again.
